title: Game World — Structs
description: Organize the game data using structs. Define a Room struct with fields: name (string), description (string), and exits for north/south/east/west (int index, -1 if no exit). Define an Item struct with fields: name, type (weapon/potion/key), and value. Define a Player struct with fields: name, health, gold, attackPower, currentRoom (int), and an items array. Create an array of Room structs to build a small map (at least 4 rooms). Use dot notation to access struct members throughout the game. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.
test_code:
assert cpp.count('struct') >= 3, f'Define at least 3 structs (Room, Item, Player) — found {cpp.count("struct")}'
assert 'Room' in cpp, 'Define a Room struct'
assert 'Item' in cpp, 'Define an Item struct'
assert 'Player' in cpp, 'Define a Player struct'
import re
dot_access = re.findall(r'\w+\.\w+', cpp)
assert len(dot_access) >= 5, 'Use dot notation to access struct members (e.g., player.health, rooms[0].name)'
assert 'north' in cpp or 'exits' in cpp, 'Room struct should have exit directions (north, south, etc.)'
assert 'name' in cpp and 'health' in cpp and 'gold' in cpp, 'Player struct should have name, health, and gold fields'
acceptance_criteria: Room struct defined with name, description, and exit directions
Item struct defined with name, type, and value
Player struct defined with name, health, gold, attackPower, currentRoom, and inventory
An array of Room structs creates a map of at least 4 rooms
Rooms are connected via exit indices (e.g., rooms[0].north = 1)
Dot notation used throughout to access struct members
hints: Use -1 for exits that don't exist: rooms[0].west = -1; means no west exit
Access nested data: rooms[player.currentRoom].name gives the current room's name
Pass structs to functions: void displayRoom(Room room) { cout << room.name << endl; }
